data.title: Senate panel backs Paul Atkins as Trump's SEC pick
data.description:
The U.S. Senate Banking Committee voted Thursday to advance President Donald Trump's nominees to lead the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Office of the Comptroller of the Currency.

In a hearing, the panel voted 13-11 in a party-line vote to approve Paul Atkins as SEC chair and Jonathan Gould as OCC chief.

Now, the nominations await full Senate approval, which would allow Atkins, a former SEC commissioner, and Gould, a former chief counsel at the OCC, to start leading their respective agencies.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: OpenAI makes ChatGPT Plus free for college students in US, Canada
data.description:
OpenAI has made ChatGPT Plus free for college students residing in the U.S. and Canada for a limited time.

"ChatGPT Plus is free for college students in the US and Canada through May!," CEO Sam Altman posted on X today.  

A subscription to ChatGPT Plus costs $20 per month, while ChatGPT Pro goes for $200 per month. The offering might allow the Microsoft-backed (NASDAQ:MSFT) startup to reel in more paying subscribers once the free period ends.  

ChatGPT, the generative artificial intelligence chatbot, has added new paid subscribers at a quick clip, as it continues to add new tools and add-ons to its flagship product. Its latest image generation tool, released last week, prompted a swarm of new users eager to download the app. Global app downloads and weekly active users on the app grew 11% and 5%, respectively, from the prior week, while in-app purchase revenue increased 6%. ChatGPT also surpassed 150M weekly active users for the first time this year.

ChatGPT also recently surpassed 20M paid subscribers. That's a three-month jump of 4.5M paid subscribers, or roughly 30%. This suggests the company is generating at least $415M in revenue per month, up from at least $333M per month at the end of last year. OpenAI expects its revenue to more than triple in 2025 and reach $12.7B.

OpenAI's recent $40B funding round, which was done in partnership with SoftBank Group (OTCPK:SFTBY), has given the company a $300B valuation. However, Softbank introduced a caveat requiring OpenAI to transition to a for-profit company by the end of 2025 to secure all the new funding, according to reports.

data.title: Maersk says Trump tariffs will hurt global trade, delay goods in transit
data.description:
Shipping company A.P. Moeller-Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKBF) warned Thursday that the sweeping tariffs announced by the Trump administration will hurt the global economy and delay goods currently in transit, Dow Jones reported.

Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKBF) said it expects customers - which include top retailers such as Amazon, Walmart and Target - will be more cautious about inventory levels and delay clearing goods through customs as they wait for clarity on the tariffs.

Trump's move to slap a minimum 10% tariff on most goods imported into the U.S. as well as much higher levies on dozens of other countries "clearly isn't good news for [the] global economy, stability and trade," Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKBF) said, but it is "still too early to say with any confidence how this ultimately will unfold."

"In the very short term, it is likely we will see an increase in demand for bonded storage as customers will want to hold off clearing goods while they get more certainty," the company said.

Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKBF) shares slid more than 9% in Copenhagen on Thursday, and European shipping peer Hapag-Lloyd (OTCPK:HLAGF) (OTCPK:HPGLY) fell 9% in Frankfurt.

Separately, Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP) said on Wednesday it and Lanco Group said it agreed to sell the 47-mile Panama Canal Railway Company to a Maersk (OTCPK:AMKBY) (OTCPK:AMKBF) unit; financial terms were not disclosed.

President Trump has complained for months about Chinese influence of the Panama Canal, and the deal somewhat undermines the U.S. presence along the key trade route.

data.title: Twilio in focus on report of data hack impacting nearly 850K customers
data.description:
• Twilio (NYSE:TWLO) was in focus on Thursday after HackRead reported that the communications software suffered a data breach that saw the data records of 848,000 of its customers sold.
  • The breach occurred at Twilio's SendGrid, which is a cloud-based email delivery platform, the news outlet added.
  • Data that may have been impacted include customer emails, phone numbers, physical addresses, as well as certain company-level data, such as revenue, employee counts and rankings. Other pertinent financial information, such as operating income, and net income may have also been impacted.
  • Twilio shares fell more than 9% on Thursday as part of a broader market sell off in response to President Trump's tariffs.

data.title: Tariff uncertainty suggests Fed can 'afford to be patient but attentive' on rates, Cook says
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook said Thursday that the U.S. central bank "can afford to be patient but attentive" on interest-rate changes, as heightened uncertainty around federal government policies clouds the economic outlook.

“Amid growing uncertainty and risks to both sides of our dual mandate, I believe it will be appropriate to maintain the policy rate at its current level while continuing to vigilantly monitor developments that could change the outlook,” Cook said in prepared remarks delivered at a University of Pittsburgh event.

If uncertainty clears over time and inflation progress resumes, "it will likely be appropriate to lower the policy rate to reduce the degree of monetary policy restriction," she said. For now, though, monetary policy is "well situated to respond to developments, and I am continuously updating my outlook as matters evolve."

The Fed governor noted the economy kicked off the year on a solid footing, though inflation is expected to remain sticky over the near term, "in part because of tariffs and other policy changes."

Her speech comes a day after President Donald Trump announced new tariffs that the U.S. is enacting on imports from a barrage of trading partners.

Cook noted that tariff hikes usually drive up prices for the impacted goods, which temporarily boosts national inflation. "But what matters for monetary policy would be a persistent boost to inflation," she added, noting "I am carefully watching various channels through which tariff effects could have more widespread implications for prices."

data.title: Fitch Ratings warns that Southwest Airlines' new baggage fee policy could backfire
data.description:
Fitch Ratings lowered its outlook on Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) to Negative from Stable. The rating agency said the cut reflects concerns over Southwest’s potential shift toward a less conservative financial policy and the risks posed by recent strategic changes, such as introducing bag fees and expiring flight credits. 

Based on Fitch's analysis, the moves, aimed at boosting profitability, could erode Southwest's (NYSE:LUV) competitive strengths relative to network carriers. Fitch also warned that failure to achieve margin improvements or increased leverage due to higher capital deployment could prompt a downgrade.

Fitch said it expects some margin recovery from current low levels, though the ultimate impact of strategic changes remains uncertain. The airline estimates operating profits could exceed $4 billion by 2026, but Fitch adopts a more conservative forecast due to potential customer pushback and economic uncertainties.

On a broad view, Fitch said the evolution of Southwest's (LUV) financial policy is a key concern. Recent developments, including a new CFO appointment, board influence from activist investors, and an accelerated share repurchase program, were highlighted as suggesting a shift toward a less cautious approach. While Southwest (LUV) remains committed to maintaining an investment-grade rating, its flexibility on leverage targets could raise questions with the rating agency about future financial discipline.

On the macro front, economic headwinds add further uncertainty for the airline company. Slower consumer demand and reduced government spending may challenge Southwest's (LUV) efforts to restore profitability. Fitch views Southwest's (LUV) fleet monetization as credit-positive if used for debt reduction or capital costs but warns against excessive shareholder returns or asset depletion.

Shares of Southwest Airlines (LUV) were down 8.8% in afternoon trading on Thursday amid a broad market decline tied to concerns over tariffs and recession risk.

data.title: W.W. Grainger downgraded at Wolfe Research on tariffs, slowing momentum
data.description:
W.W. Grainger (NYSE:GWW) on Thursday was downgraded to Underperform from a previous investment rating of Peer Perform by analysts at Wolfe Research. They said the industrial supplier faces a "tough setup" heading into its first-quarter earnings report and a challenging path forward under the new U.S. tariff regime.

“While the stock has traded heavy in recent weeks, we are growing increasingly cautious on the FY25 guidance framework, which embeds no impact for tariffs,” Ryan Cooke, analyst at Wolfe, said an April 3 report.

He said Grainger (NYSE:GWW) has 35% to 40% import exposure on U.S. cost of goods sold, and believes that the newly imposed tariffs could compress margins, pushing earnings toward the low end of the company’s fiscal 2025 guidance range. Wolfe’s own EPS forecast now sits at $39.83, below the mid-range of management’s $39 to $41.50 guidance and implying 2% to 3% downside to consensus expectations over the rest of the year.

Pricing, private labels and policy risk

One key concern is the timing and cadence of Grainger’s (GWW) price increases, which are scheduled three times annually. With the next round set for May 1, the company will need to carefully calibrate price hikes against a rapidly evolving economic backdrop.

Grainger’s (GWW) reliance on private-label products, which carry higher margins but are more import-dependent, also poses a risk. These products represent roughly 20% of total sales and earn 10 percentage points higher gross margins than branded goods. In a tariff-heavy environment, these goods may become less price-competitive, impacting both sales and profitability.

Cooke also questioned the sustainability of Grainger’s (GWW) long-term goal to outgrow the market by 400 to 500 basis points, a target set in 2022 when the company benefited from pandemic-driven supply chain advantages and strong e-commerce demand. With competitive dynamics normalizing and the company missing the mark in each of the last three quarters, Wolfe sees further downside risk.

Additionally, Cooke flagged government-related exposure — particularly from the Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE — as another vulnerability. Government entities make up about 16% of Grainger’s (GWW) sales, primarily at the state and local level, but budget constraints could disproportionately affect the company compared to peers.

Valuation not compelling

While Grainger’s (GWW) valuation has moderated in recent months, Cooke noted that the stock still trades at roughly 24 times next-twelve-months price-to-earnings ratio, above its five-year median of about 20 times. Wolfe’s fair value estimate implies 4% downside, but Cooke warned that the risk could exceed 20% if the stock reverts to historical multiples in the high teens.

“Bottom line, there are many hurdles to overcome and risk/reward is heavily out of kilter from here,” Cooke concluded.

Wolfe also upgraded Fastenal (FAST) to Peer Perform, citing a more balanced risk/reward profile relative to Grainger (GWW). The researcher downgraded Parker-Hannifin (PH) and Hayward Holdings (HAYW) to Peer Perform.

data.title: Nasdaq-100 plunges 5% and these 10 holdings have fallen the hardest
data.description:
Trump's latest tariff announcement has sent tremors through the market, triggering a significant sell-off and pushing the Nasdaq-100 (NDX) into a steep decline, with the index dropping 5% on Thursday. The tech-heavy index has suffered broad losses, with 80 of its 100 constituent stocks trading in the red. 

A major contributor to the downturn has been Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP), whose shares have plummeted 16.9% on the day. Below are the top 10 worst-performing Nasdaq-100 stocks of the day.

No. 10: Atlassian Corporation (NASDAQ:TEAM) -10.8%.

No. 9: AppLovin Corporation (NASDAQ:APP) -10.9%.

No. 8: Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) -11.2%.

No. 7: ON Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:ON) -11.3%.

No. 6: MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) -11.5%.

No. 5: Dianmondback Energy (NASDAQ:FANG) -11.8%.

No. 4:Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) -12.1%.

No. 3: The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) -12.3%.

No. 2: Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) -14.4%.

No. 1: Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) -16.9%.

Prices are reflected as of 2:30 pm EST.

Moreover, see how Seeking Alpha’s quant grades list the 10 above names: TEAM 3.34, APP 3.49, SBUX 3.20, ON 2.64, MSTR 3.32, FANG 3.15, WBD 3.44, TTD 1.39, MU 4.31, and MCHP 1.65.

As the Nasdaq-100 tumbles, so too does Wall Street's fifth-largest exchange-traded fund. The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ), with $299.80B in assets under management, has also fallen by 4.9%. 

Furthermore, see some other Nasdaq-100 (NDX) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) focused exchange traded funds: (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).

data.title: UnitedHealth must face Medicare fraud case: DoJ says
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Justice ((DoJ)) has urged a federal judge to proceed with a long-running fraud lawsuit alleging that UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) overbilled the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) by at least $2.1B.

The DOJ’s proposal came at a federal court on Wednesday after a special master assigned to the case argued in March that the agency lacked evidence to back up its fraud allegations. UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) had until Apr. 2 to respond.

The company is expected to respond to the DOJ by May 2 as the case, initially filed by whistleblower Benjamin Poehling, a former UNH staffer, more than a decade ago, continues.

In 2017, the DoJ took over the case, which alleges that UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), the leading player in the market for government-backed Medicare Advantage plans, failed to delete unsupported diagnosis codes from patient records, leading to as much as $2.1B in overpayments for its MA plans.

data.title: Trump’s tariffs are not priced in, inflation could rise to 5% – UBS
data.description:
UBS’s Tariff Fear gauge, which is measured on a scale of 0-100, recorded 11%, down from 30% in March, for U.S. equities (SP500), meaning tariffs could not be priced in, according to UBS.

European equities (VGK), (IEV), (FEZ) were at 46% this month as of Wednesday.

This fear gauge calculates returns of U.S. and European tariff-affected companies, not explained by changes in macro variables such as growth, real rates, credit spreads or inflation expectations, UBS analysts explained.

“Essentially, until yesterday, the markets were thinking of tariffs purely as a negotiating tool, not an ideological one,” said Bhanu Baweja, chief strategist at UBS. “Indeed, there is a chance that rates of reciprocal tariffs do come lower as trading partners negotiate with the U.S., but the 10% universal tariff suggests this administration firmly believes tariffs are the policy needed to fundamentally alter trade and economic relationships with the global economy.”

He added that the hope for these tariffs being negotiated down are immediately challenged by the possibility of retaliation from the U.S.’s trading partners and how difficult it is for countries to negotiate lowering other non-tariff barriers, including domestic taxes, currency levels and other regulations.

“Trump's proposals are likely to raise the weighted average tariffs on U.S. imports from 2.5% as of end 2024 to 24% – levels not seen since the 1920s,” Baweja said. “Both the magnitude of ‘reciprocal’ tariffs imposed per country and the set of countries they were imposed on were larger than many had reasonably anticipated.”

The broad 10% tariffs imposition has “the potential to considerably worsen the growth inflation mix in the U.S. and the global economy in the coming year,” he said.

UBS economic team projects the U.S.’s 2025 real GDP to lower by 1.5-2 percentage points and inflation to reach 5% if these tariffs are not reversed soon.

“The magnitude of damage they could cause to the U.S. economy makes one’s rational mind regard the possibility of them sticking as low.”
